problem text: Imagine performing a linear regression on a dataset to find the coefficients $\hat{\beta}_{OLS}$. Remember that if $X$ represents the data and $\sigma^2$ denotes the variance of the identically distributed normal errors, then $\text{Var}\left(\hat{\beta}_{OLS}\right) = \sigma^2(X^TX)^{-1}$. If you conduct linear regression again on the data where each value is doubled from your initial dataset, resulting in new coefficients $\hat{\beta}_{OLS}'$, determine the constant $c$ such that $\text{Var}\left(\hat{\beta}_{OLS}'\right) = c\text{Var}\left(\hat{\beta}_{OLS}\right)$. If there is no such constant, input $-1$.
problem solution: Upon doubling the data, we substitute the matrix $X$ with the matrix $X' = 2X$. Additionally, $\sigma$ is replaced with $2\sigma$. Consequently, $X'^TX' = 4X^TX$ through straightforward matrix multiplication. Hence, $(2\sigma)^2 (4X^T X)^{-1} = \frac{1}{4} \cdot 4 \cdot \sigma^2 (X^TX)^{-1} = \sigma^2 (X^TX)^{-1}$, which confirms our conclusion.
problem hint: Doubling the data implies substituting the matrix $X$ with the matrix $X' = 2X$. Consequently, $X'^TX' = 4X^TX$ through straightforward matrix multiplication. Consider the effect on $\sigma$?
